content.full_text:
RAVI MEHTA
CHAIRMAN

FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION

May 22, 1995


John R. Banuelos, Director
Dept. of Boating & Waterways
1629 S Street
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re: Your Request for Advice
    Our File No. A-95-145

Dear Mr. Banuelos:

     This is in response to your request for advice under the
conflict-of-interest and financial disclosure provisions of the
Political Reform Act.1/ Some of the facts were provided in your
written request for advice as well as in our May 9, 1995 telephone
conversation. Note that this advice is limited to the obligations
of public officials from the State of California.

QUESTIONS

     If the San Diego Port Commission sponsors a dinner or
luncheon for participants at the 1995 Western States Boating
Administrators Association conference, would this:

     1. Create a conflict of interest between the Department of
Boating and Waterways and the San Diego Port Commission?

     2. Create any problems if public officials attend the dinner
or luncheon free of charge?

     3. Require any disclosure of the receipt of the gift of the
dinner or luncheon?




     1/ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations,
Sections 18000-18954. All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.


428 J STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814 (916) 322-5660

File No. A-95-145
Page 2


CONCLUSIONS

1. The Act does not regulate potential conflicts of interest
between governmental bodies. The conflict-of-interest provisions
of the Act only apply to public officials who may be prohibited
from participating in decisions which may affect their personal
economic interests.

2. If the value of gifts over a 12 month period from the San
Diego Port Commission to an official amounts to $280 or more, the
officials may be prohibited from making or participating in the
making of any decisions which will have a material financial
effect on the San Diego Port Commission.

3. If public officials accept gifts from the San Diego Port
Commission valued at $50 or more, those officials may be required
to disclose the gift on their Statements of Economic Interests.

FACTS

You are the Director of the Department of Boating and
Waterways. You are also the vice-president of the Western States
Boating Administrators Association ("WSBAA".) The WSBAA is an
association of 13 western states that meet to promote boating
safety. As the vice-president, you are responsible for
coordinating the 1995 WSBAA conference in San Diego. This
conference is scheduled for July 16 through 19.

As part of the conference, the WSBAA will have an awards
banquet for conference participants. You indicated that the
participants at the banquet were expected to fully pay for their
attendance at the banquet. The cost to each attendee would be
between $17 and $22, depending on the meal item ordered. The San
Diego Port Commission ("Port Commission") has offered to pick up
the tab for the banquet.2/

The Port Commission is a governmental agency which receives
loans and grants from the Department of Boating and Waterways.
You have asked what problems, if any, would occur if the Port
Commission sponsors this awards banquet.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits public officials from making,
participating in, or using their official position to influence a

_______________________
2/ There may be the additional question of whether such an
expenditure by the Port Commission would be considered a legal
expenditure. (See, e.g. Penal Code Section 424; Stanson v. Mott
(1976) 17 Cal. 3d 206. Penal Code Section 424 which deals with
the misuse of public moneys, are outside the jurisdiction of the
Commission. Penal Code Section 424 is interpreted and enforced by
the district attorney and the Attorney General.

File No. A-95-145
Page 3


governmental decision in which they know or have reason to know
they have a financial interest.

An official has a financial interest in a governmental
decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial
effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally,
on the official, or on a member of the official's immediate
family, or on:

* * *

(e) Any donor of, or any intermediary or agent for
a donor of, a gift or gifts aggregating two hundred
fifty dollars ($250) or more in value provided to,
received by, or promised to the public official within
12 months prior to the time when the decision is made.
The amount of the value of gifts specified by this
subdivision shall be adjusted biennially by the
commission to equal the same amount determined by the
commission pursuant to subdivision (d) of Section
89504.3/

The term "gift" as used above means any payment to the extent
that consideration of equal or greater value is not received and
includes a rebate or discount in the price of anything of value
unless the rebate or discount is made in the regular course of
business to members of the public without regard to official
status.4/

Accordingly, if the Port Commission picks up the tab for the
awards banquet and the value of gifts from the Port Commission
equal or exceed $280 or more over a 12-month period to an
official, the official could not make or participate in the making
of any governmental decision which will have a material financial
effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally,
on the Port Commission.5/

In addition to the conflict-of-interest provisions of the
Act, public officials may also have an obligation to disclose the
receipt of this gift.  Every governmental agency in the State of

3/ Effective January 1, 1995, that amount was adjusted to $280.

4/ The term "gifts" includes gifts of food or beverage.  (Section
82030.)

5/ Should you personally receive gifts from the San Diego Port
Commission which equal or exceed $280 in a calendar year, we
request you seek advice concerning your ability to participate in
governmental decisions which may affect the San Diego Port
Commission.

File No. A-95-145
Page 4


California is required to develop a conflict of interest code.
This code specifies who within the agency must disclose their
personal economic interests and further specifies what economic
interests must be reported. For example, as director for the
Department of Boating and Waterways, your agency's conflict of
interest code requires you to disclose all interests in real
property, income6/ and your status as a director, officer,
partner, trustee, employee or holder of any position of management
in any business entity. Thus, you would be required to disclose
the Port Commission as the source of a gift if valued at $50 or
more.7/ Other public officials from California governmental
agencies must refer to their agency's conflict of interest code to
determine their disclosure obligations.

You indicated that the value of attendance to the awards
banquet is expected to be approximately $17 to $22 per person. If
the value of that gift, as well as the value of other gifts
received from the Port Commission, do not exceed $50 or more, the
receipt of this gift would not require disclosure for any one
public official, nor form the basis for disqualification.

I trust this provides the guidance you sought. If you have
additional questions, or wish to discuss this further, please feel
free to call me at (916) 322-5901.

Sincerely,

Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel

By: Jeanette E. Turvill
Political Reform Consultant
Legal Division

SGC/JET/jt


6/ The term "income" means, among other things, gifts, including
gifts of food or beverage. (Section 82030.)

7/ Note that there are certain exceptions to the definition of
gift, such as gifts from family members, etc. Refer to Form 730
and your conflict of interest code for additional exceptions.
